title: Fitting a window lock to a timber sash window
subtitle: Install a sash stop, screw lock, or dual-screw to prevent a sash window opening more than a ventilation gap
excerpt: How to fit a security lock to a timber sash window. Covers three locking options (sash stop, sash screw, and dual-screw) and the installation method for each, including drilling, fitting, and keying for insurance compliance.
sourceNotes: UK window-security trade practice. Insurance industry guidance on sash window locking. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: A sash window lock consisting of a threaded brass bolt that screws through the lower sash rail into a captive nut fitted to the upper sash or inner face. When the bolt is screwed home the two sash frames are locked together; removing the bolt allows the window to open. Dual-screws require a special key (a hexagonal or star-shaped bit) to operate. Most household insurers accept dual-screws as an approved sash-window security fitting when fitted to both sides of the window.

content.text: A timber sash window without a security lock can be forced open silently. Three products address this: a sash stop (a hardened pin that limits travel), a sash screw (surface-mounted, key-operated bolt), and a
(recessed bolt through both sashes). Dual-screws give the best security and the most discreet fit; sash stops are the easiest to fit.

A sash stop is a surface-mounted wedge or pin that screws to the face of the lower sash and blocks the upper sash from travelling past it. Position the stop at the closed position for a locking fit, or 100 to 150 mm up from the bottom rail for a ventilation position. Drill pilot holes and screw the stop to the lower sash with the screws provided. To lock: slide the stop to the block position. No key required.

Close the window fully. Mark the drill position on the lower sash rail 30 to 40 mm from the meeting rail. Drill through the lower sash with the bit supplied (typically 10 mm) angled slightly downward so the bolt will clear the upper sash rebate. Continue until the bit just marks the upper sash. Remove the bit; insert the captive nut into the hole in the upper sash with the nut tool supplied. Screw the bolt through the lower sash hole into the nut. Both sides should be fitted (left and right) for insurance approval.

For a dual-screw ventilation position, drill a second set of holes in the lower sash only, 100 mm above the first. Fitting the bolt in the upper holes locks the window open 100 mm (ventilation without a wide enough gap for entry. Fit captive nuts at both heights.

attrs.symptom: The dual-screw bolt meets resistance before fully home and the window is not tight
attrs.cause: The captive nut in the upper sash is not seated perpendicular to the hole) it is rotated or tilted.
attrs.fix: Remove the bolt and re-seat the captive nut using the nut-setting tool, pressing firmly until the nut flanges are flush with the wood surface.
